name: Lobizon
description: The Lobizón is a werewolf-like creature from Argentine and Latin American folklore. According to legend, the seventh son born in a family will become a Lobizón, transforming into a wolf-like beast during full moons.
appearance: Lobizon is known from recorded folklore and oral tradition in Argentina.
behavior: In traditional accounts, Lobizon from Americas  plays its role in the mythology and folklore of its culture.
cultural_significance: The Lobizón myth is related to European werewolf legends but adapted to the Southern Cone. In Argentina, the belief was so persistent that a law was passed allowing the President to become godfather of seventh sons to ward off the curse.
summary: The Lobizón is a werewolf-like creature from Argentine and Latin American folklore. According to legend, the seventh son born in a family will become a Lobizón, transforming into a wolf-like beast during full moons.
fun_fact: A werewolf figure from Argentina mythology.
source: Latin American folklore; Argentine legal tradition

name: El Chupacabra
description: El Chupacabra (Spanish for "goat-sucker") is a legendary cryptid first reported in Puerto Rico in 1995. Described as a reptile-like creature with spines along its back, it allegedly attacks livestock by draining their blood.
appearance: El Chupacabra is known from recorded folklore and oral tradition in Puerto Rico.
behavior: In traditional accounts, El Chupacabra from Americas  plays its role in the mythology and folklore of its culture.
cultural_significance: The Chupacabra is a relatively modern legend that became a pan-Latin American phenomenon. It reflects anxieties about rural life and has been analyzed as a case of mass hysteria and folkloric narrative creation in the modern era.
summary: El Chupacabra (Spanish for "goat-sucker") is a legendary cryptid first reported in Puerto Rico in 1995. Described as a reptile-like creature with spines along its back, it allegedly attacks livestock by draining their blood.
fun_fact: A cryptid figure from Puerto Rico mythology.
source: Contemporary Latin American folklore; news reports (1995–present)

name: Zombi
description: In Haitian Vodou tradition, a zombi is a reanimated corpse brought back to life by a bokor (sorcerer). Unlike the modern cinematic version, the Haitian zombi is a mindless laborer under the control of its creator.
appearance: Zombi is known from recorded folklore and oral tradition in Haiti.
behavior: In traditional accounts, Zombi from Americas  plays its role in the mythology and folklore of its culture.
cultural_significance: The zombi originates from Haitian Vodou and is deeply tied to the history of slavery on the island. It was legally recognized in Haiti's 19th-century criminal code. The concept entered global pop culture through films like White Zombie (1932).
summary: In Haitian Vodou tradition, a zombi is a reanimated corpse brought back to life by a bokor (sorcerer). Unlike the modern cinematic version, the Haitian zombi is a mindless laborer under the control of its creator.
fun_fact: A undead figure from Haiti mythology.
source: The Serpent and the Rainbow (Davis, 1985); Haitian Folklore (Courlander, 1960)

name: Guije
description: Güije (also spelled Jigüe) is a creature from Afro-Cuban folklore, a small, playful, sometimes mischievous black figure that lives near rivers and streams. It is known for splashing water at passersby and appearing to children.
appearance: Guije is known from recorded folklore and oral tradition in Cuba.
behavior: In traditional accounts, Guije from Americas  plays its role in the mythology and folklore of its culture.
summary: Güije (also spelled Jigüe) is a creature from Afro-Cuban folklore, a small, playful, sometimes mischievous black figure that lives near rivers and streams. It is known for splashing water at passersby and appearing to children.
fun_fact: A water-spirit figure from Cuba mythology.

name: La Malinche
description: Malintzin (also known as Malinche or Doña Marina) is a controversial figure in Mexican history and folklore. She served as interpreter, advisor, and intermediary for Hernán Cortés during the Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire.
appearance: La Malinche is known from recorded folklore and oral tradition in Mexico.
behavior: In traditional accounts, La Malinche from Americas  plays its role in the mythology and folklore of its culture.
cultural_significance: La Malinche is a complex and polarizing figure in Mexican cultural identity, viewed variously as a traitor, a victim, and the symbolic mother of the mestizo Mexican people. Her legacy remains deeply contested.
summary: Malintzin (also known as Malinche or Doña Marina) is a controversial figure in Mexican history and folklore. She served as interpreter, advisor, and intermediary for Hernán Cortés during the Spanish conquest of the Aztec Empire.
fun_fact: A human figure from Mexico mythology.
